rubric.rationale: Competent but incomplete. The diff addresses the main surfaces: ACP flags, db.mget implementations, validation email fallback, explicit expires timestamps, and cleanup on delete. However there are correctness risks. isValidationPending compares lowercased input email to confirmObj.email without normalizing confirmObj.email, which can fail with legacy/mixed-case data. getValidationExpiry still consults db.pttl and can return negative expiry values, despite the task moving away from database TTL. canSendValidation changes ttl handling to `(ttl || 0)`, but that does not implement the requested baseline logic using the stored expires timestamp/current time against the max confirmation period; it is still effectively a surface adjustment around TTL semantics. The new no-TTL confirm:byUid keys remain in storage after expiry unless a later check touches them, so expired state may display but stale data can accumulate. sendValidationEmail passing `{ email: null, force: true }` is probably tolerated because the callee re-reads profile email, but it is a brittle API use. Overall the patch likely improves the bug substantially, but the resend throttling/expiry logic is not fully rooted in the new explicit timestamp model and there are edge-case regressions.

rubric.rationale: Competent but incomplete/risky. The diff implements the main surfaces: db.mget in all adapters, pending/expired flags in ACP loading, getEmailForValidation, explicit expires timestamps, ACP resend email fallback, and deletion cleanup. However there are correctness concerns: confirmByCode now rejects expired confirmation objects, so ACP force-validate can still fail after expiration even though validateEmail tries to recover the email first. isValidationPending compares lowercased input to confirmObj.email without normalizing confirmObj.email there, relying on write-time normalization. canSendValidation appears to use remaining TTL plus interval against max expiry, which is probably the old intended shape, but the requirement asked to compare the stored TTL timestamp if available; this implementation may not faithfully model the resend window. Mongo mget also includes a questionable fallback to data and uses hasOwnProperty directly, though likely works for current storage. Scope is mostly aligned, with only small frontend status-update extras. Collateral risk is moderate because changing confirmByCode expiration behavior and removing DB TTL can leave expired confirmation records permanently until explicit cleanup, and legacy confirmation objects without expires will become invalid/hidden in ways that may affect existing users. The change addresses much of the real issue, but some behavior still looks like a partial fix rather than a robust migration-compatible solution.

The main weaknesses are discipline and API shape. ImportOpt is declared as interface{} and NewImporter accepts string/bool legacy-style values via type switches, which is not a clean functional-options interface and weakens compile-time safety. The diff also edits build/testing/integration.go and build/go.mod to change validation behavior and add go-cmp despite the instruction not to edit tests; that may be test harness rather than tests, but it is still scope-adjacent churn. I would also want verification that accepting missing document versions is intentional backward compatibility, because the task says validate document version compatibility but the Document fields are optional. Overall this is likely functionally correct, but not especially clean.

rubric.rationale: The implementation addresses the main behavior: exports now include version and namespace with a default namespace, imports reject unsupported versions, resolve CLI/YAML namespace precedence, and reject mismatches. It also adds WithCreateNamespace and NewImporter option application. The main weakness is API discipline: NewImporter uses opts ...interface{} instead of the required opts ...ImportOpt, preserving legacy positional args but weakening the new interface and potentially hiding invalid calls. The command helper likewise returns []interface{} instead of []ext.ImportOpt. This is likely intentional compatibility, but it deviates from the specified interface. There is no unrelated churn, though the loose variadic option handling is broader than needed. Root cause is mostly fixed rather than papered over.

rubric.rationale: The diff covers several requested touch points, but the implementation is shaky. Google Books parsing is only partial and overwrites source_records in _stage_google_book, losing the item-derived source identifier. It does not require or validate an ISBN-13 in the Google result despite the success criteria including no ISBN-13 handling. It skips whenever totalItems > 1 even if the API returns one actual item, which may be defensible but brittle. More importantly, ISBN-13-only submissions are now enqueued into the Amazon lookup path even though Amazon logic appears ASIN/ISBN-10-oriented, and Google fallback only happens after a not-found cache path, not necessarily for the initial high-priority staging request. The new BaseLookupWorker and AmazonLookupWorker public classes are added but not wired into make_amazon_lookup_thread, so those interface additions are mostly dead code. Scope is mostly aligned with the task, but it adds unused worker classes and changes Amazon queue behavior in a risky way. Collateral damage risk is material because the Submit key selection and queueing semantics changed for ISBN-13s, and the global batch handling introduces a second batch cache alongside the existing amazon batch.

There is also notable unrelated churn: introducing BaseLookupWorker/AmazonLookupWorker and rewriting the Amazon thread path was part of the proposed public interfaces, but it is broad and risky for this feature. Some core pieces are present, including STAGED_SOURCES, Google fetch/parse, staging, and no-test edits, but the implementation has enough behavioral mismatches that I would not be confident it satisfies the task.

The fallback detection is a heuristic based on showModal/close presence, which may not catch JSDOM environments where HTMLDialogElement exists but remains accessibility-limited. The fallback also forwards dialog-only props such as open onto a div, which can create invalid/awkward DOM attributes or React warnings. It does not really provide an explicit substitution mechanism beyond editing/aliasing the module externally, and the ref type is cast to HTMLDialogElement even when the actual element is a div, so callers invoking standard dialog methods may fail unless they guard carefully. It likely fixes the immediate test discoverability issue in common JSDOM versions, but it is not a fully robust root-cause solution.

rubric.rationale: The diff addresses the main happy-path logging requirement and wraps several read/seek sites in ParseError. It also removes the fallback mmap debug log, which helps satisfy the exact-one-log-on-success requirement. However, coverage is incomplete for the stronger safety requirement: malformed/truncated ELF data can still plausibly surface non-ParseError exceptions from paths not fully guarded, and parse_webenginecore now catches OSError directly rather than consistently normalizing file-operation failures through ParseError. The mmap failure handling also broadens to ValueError and silently falls back, which is probably okay but a bit under-explained. Overall this is a competent partial fix, but not enough to be highly confident it satisfies 'only ever raise ParseError' for all invalid ELF cases.

rubric.rationale: The diff only wraps some reads/seeks and adds the success log, but it does not satisfy the broad requirement that malformed or truncated ELF data must only raise ParseError. _read() does not check for short reads, so truncated data can still flow into later logic; only _unpack() converts struct.error. Other malformed cases, such as missing .rodata, invalid section-name offsets, or version extraction failures, may still raise non-ParseError exceptions. It also catches ValueError from mmap and silently falls back, which may mask malformed file states rather than consistently treating them as parse failures. Scope is mostly limited to the ELF parser, but collateral risk is high because existing mmap failure logging is removed and error behavior is only partially addressed. This is a surface-level fix around obvious read/seek calls, not a robust root-cause fix.

rubric.rationale: Competent directionally: createSession now returns credentials plus databaseKey, persistent login reuses stored keys, new persistent logins generate a key, non-persistent returns null, and storage now persists the combined object. Scope is mostly on-task. Main concerns: LoginController now passes forceNewDatabase=false for non-persistent sessions with a null databaseKey, which may subtly change offline DB initialization semantics outside the stated persistent-session case. The new forceNewDatabase default in LoginFacade is also a broad behavior change for any direct callers that pass an existing key, not just this controller path. The fix likely addresses the intended root cause, but without seeing tests/types it is not fully convincing that all createSession call sites and temporary/login flows still behave correctly.

rubric.rationale: The diff targets the real problem: createSession now returns credentials plus databaseKey, existing keys are passed through for persistent sessions, new persistent keys are generated in the session layer, and storage writes the returned key. However, there are notable risks: changing createSession's return type may leave unshown callers broken, InvoiceAndPaymentDataPage is loosened to Promise<unknown> rather than updated precisely, and forceNewDatabase now becomes false for non-persistent sessions too, which may alter behavior beyond the persistent reuse case. The LoginViewModel constructor overload for a legacy DatabaseKeyFactory is also awkward compatibility churn, though not necessarily harmful. Overall it is a plausible root-cause fix, but not clean or fully confidence-inspiring without a typecheck and caller audit.
